Topic: Zucchini fritters
TT_Video_Title: If your gut’s being dramatic again, these zucchini, pea & mint fritters might just be the peace offering it needs. 🌿 They’re light, IBS-friendly, and take only 20 minutes - no weird ingredients, just good food that won’t upset your stomach. You’ll need (serves 2 / makes 6 fritters): ½ cup mint leaves, finely chopped 1 small potato (about 100g), grated 130g courgette, grated 20g Parmesan cheese, finely grated 30g green peas 1 egg 40g gluten-free flour Zest of ½ lemon Salt & pepper Olive oil for frying How to: 1️⃣ Grate the potato and courgette, then squeeze out the extra moisture with a clean tea towel. 2️⃣ Mix everything together in a bowl — potato, courgette, peas, Parmesan, mint, lemon zest, egg, flour, salt, and pepper. 3️⃣ Heat a little olive oil in a non-stick pan. Add spoonfuls of the mix and fry 3–4 mins each side until golden. Low FODMAP, easy to digest, and surprisingly good.

Topic: Salmon recpie
TT_Video_Title: Why is this recipe your go-to when managing Endometriosis? The Omega-3s in salmon work at a cellular level to calm the chronic, overactive inflammatory "fire" associated with endometriosis, which is why it is often recommended as part of an anti-inflammatory diet for managing the condition. But it's not just for 'Endo girls'! Salmon - Packed with Omega-3s Quinoa - Low-FODMAP Spinach - Magnesium-rich This is one of those meals that even people who don't spend much time in the kitchen won't struggle to make. Ingredients (2 servings) 2 salmon fillets, descaled 1 lemon (½ juiced, ½ sliced) 1 tbsp extra virgin olive oil 1 tsp dried oregano or mixed herbs 1 tsp dried dill Salt and pepper 75g dry quinoa (rinsed) 450ml water or low-FODMAP stock 4 large handfuls baby spinach Method Preheat oven to 180°C Cook quinoa in water or stock. Simmer on medium heat for 15 mins. Whilst quinoa is cooking, place salmon on a tray, drizzle with the extra virgin olive oil, lemon juice, and herbs. Add salt and pepper to taste. Lay lemon slices on top. Bake for 12–14 min until cooked. Fluff the quinoa with a fork. Stir spinach into the hot quinoa to wilt and leave to stand until the salmon is ready. Serve salmon over the quinoa-spinach mix. Thank you @dietitianfitfor this recipe!

Topic: Kiwi banana overnight oats
TT_Video_Title: We know… not the most aesthetic oats you’ll see today. But they’re rich in fibre and great for anyone struggling with digestion or constipation. Sometimes function > esthetic. Topic: 7 things women know about weight loss
Caption: Behind every “failed” diet is a body doing exactly what it’s designed to do. We spoke to one of our dietitians about women’s weight loss and the most common misconceptions.  Weight regain isn’t about willpower, it’s biology. The body fights weight loss through hormonal and metabolic adaptation, so regaining isn’t a personal failure. ✅ Truth: Your body isn’t against you. It’s just protecting you. 	 Generic meal plans fail because they ignore your life. Meal plans don’t teach you how to eat, they just give you a list. Once you get bored, you’re lost again. ✅ Truth: You can’t outsource your nutrition.  Weight loss requires emotional coping, not just calorie tracking. After bariatric surgery, people lose their go-to coping mechanism (food) and must rebuild emotional regulation. ✅ Truth: You can’t heal your body if you don’t also heal your emotions.  True support means flexibility and continuity, not quick fixes. The NHS often lacks time; private care allows sustained, tailored guidance that fits real life. ✅ Truth: Long-term change needs long-term support.  Hormonal shifts need smart nutrition, not restriction. Bariatric-style eating (high protein, balanced meals, nutrient-rich) works beautifully when adjusted to PCOS or menopause. ✅ Truth: Your hormones aren’t a dead end, they’re a roadmap.  Bariatric surgery isn’t the easy way out, it’s a lifelong commitment. Patients must relearn eating, pacing, supplementation every day. ✅ Truth: Discipline doesn’t end with surgery, it starts there.  Rebuilding trust with food takes patience Learning to tell hunger from habit, and not letting one bad day ruin progress, is key. ✅ Truth: Progress is consistency, not perfection.  If you need help with dieting, find the right nutritionist or dietitian on our website, link in bio.

Topic: Cervical cancer prevention
Caption: This week is Cervical Cancer Prevention Week — a reminder that cervical cancer is largely preventable and early detection saves lives. In the UK, around 3,200 people are diagnosed each year and roughly 850 lose their lives to it annually.   We asked Mr. Mahantesh Karoshi, MD, FRCOG — Consultant Gynaecologist in London — to answer the most common myths he hears about cervical cancer, screening, symptoms, and prevention.  ✨ Cervical cancer screening (smear tests) and the HPV vaccine are some of the most effective tools we have — and getting screened when invited can make all the difference.   💬 What Dr. Karoshi wants you to know: “Smear tests are about prevention, not judgment”  👉 Anyone with a cervix aged 25–64 is invited for regular smear tests. If that’s you - don’t skip it. Early detection saves lives.

Topic: Abbie - endo not just a bad period at times
3 therapist backed self care tool
Caption: ❤️‍🩹Pacing  - doing less (on purpose) Pacing is a cornerstone in chronic pain therapy. It’s the opposite of “push through it.” Instead, it teaches you to break activities into manageable chunks before exhaustion or pain peaks.  Pick one task, break it into 3 parts - prep, rest, finish, stop before you hit the wall.  ❤️‍🩹Grounding through sensory check-ins  Therapists teach sensory grounding to help calm pain-related anxiety and nervous system overactivation. Chronic pain keeps the brain in fight or flight, grounding helps switch it off.  Try the 5-4-3-2-1 method: Name 5 things you see 4 things you can touch 3 things you hear 2 things you smell 1 thing you taste  - great for panic attacks as well  ❤️‍🩹Self-validation journaling (not gratitude)  Many therapists use validation journaling to help you to stop gaslighting your own pain. It’s about acknowledging how hard something is without trying to fix it.  Try prompts like: Today was hard because… And that makes sense because… I’m allowed to rest because…  You don’t need to be positive, simply be you.

Topic: Aneta- debunking endo myth
Caption: We’ve come a long way, yet many are still treating Endometriosis as a minor gynecological issue. This "fertility-first" mindset isn't accidental, it’s built into the historical research that shaped modern medical training:  * 1950s (Kistner): Pioneered "Pseudo-pregnancy" research. It focused entirely on hormonal suppression of the uterus, creating the myth that pregnancy "cures" the disease.  * 1981 (B&B Scale): This was the standard for clinical trials. It only measured pain during periods and s€x. If a patient had bowel, bladder, or nerve pain, it literally wasn't on the map.  * 1985 (AFS Staging): The Stage I-IV system many doctors still use. It’s a "fertility scorecard" that assigns points for reproductive organ damage, but zero points for a patient's level of pain.  While science now recognizes Endo as a systemic, whole-body inflammatory condition, the medical "map" is often still stuck in the 80s.   This is why non-reproductive symptoms are still frequently dismissed today.

Topic: endo is as common as asthma and diabetes
Caption: Endometriosis is as common as asthma or diabetes. So why don't we talk about it?  Endometriosis affects 1 in 10 women, that’s around 1.5 million people in the UK alone, roughly the same number affected by diabetes or asthma. And yet, 54% of people don't know what it is.  If half the population had never heard of asthma, we'd consider it a public health crisis.   It causes chronic pain, fatigue, and infertility and in many cases takes years to diagnose because symptoms are so often dismissed or normalised. The condition costs the UK economy £8.2 billion a year in treatment, healthcare, and lost work.  Research shows the economic burden is comparable to or higher than other chronic diseases like heart disease and diabetes.   The comparison to asthma or diabetes isn't just about numbers. It's about the gap between how seriously we take those conditions versus how endometriosis has historically been treated: as something to push through, not something to treat.  Awareness is especially low among young women. 62% of women aged 16–24 don't know what endometriosis is. The same group that is most likely to be experiencing their first symptoms and dismissing them as "bad periods."  Endometriosis deserves the same research funding, clinical urgency, and public awareness as any other chronic condition affecting millions of lives.

Topic: amy- i need help finding an endo specialist
Caption: After my recent update regarding my endometriosis care under the NHS, I'm now debating whether I should go down the private route.   I've had so many recommendations of both surgeons and hospitals that I should look at around the globe! However I honestly don't know where to start with it all.   But then I discovered @docmapuk and thought I'd ask for their help.   DocMap offer a free WhatsApp based service to help women, like myself, find the right private specialist surgeon without the hours of research.   The service itself is quick, free and reliable with an actual person on the other end of the chat...no bots or AI!  They take away the stress and overwhelming feelings that can come with this type of research. And honestly anything that saves us energy whilst living with Endometriosis is a win right?   If you're looking into private healthcare, or just curious to see what options they could find for you, I've popped the link in my bio where you can message them directly.   And remember, it's completely free!  I now basically have my whole surgery team lined up and in place ready for when the time comes!🙌🏼  #docmap #docmapuk #docmapukpartner #endometriosis #womenshealth
